original_abstract:
This book investigates the significant political influence wielded by "weak" presidents, whether directly or indirectly elected. While traditional scholarship distinguishes presidential strength primarily through constitutional mandates, this study shifts the focus toward the nuances of political practice. Through a comparative analysis of six Western European nations, the book examines two distinct constitutional designs: the consistent models of Germany and Italy—where weak presidents are indirectly elected—and the inconsistent models of Austria, Ireland, Finland, and Portugal, where weak presidents hold a direct popular mandate.

Moving beyond formal legalistic interpretations, the book prioritizes the role of informal powers in presidential activism. It establishes a robust theoretical framework by providing an innovative typology and definition of informal power, followed by a rigorous empirical investigation into its exercise. By adopting an interactionist approach that bridges presidency-centered and president-centered explanations, the research evaluates the success of these informal interventions. Ultimately, the book argues that while the prevailing opportunity structure is a vital factor in presidential activism, a president’s level of public support remains the most critical determinant of their political efficacy.
humanized_abstract:
Book summary view The Politics of Weak Presidents: Directly and Indirectly Elected explores the political influence that presidents have when they are classified as weak, directly or indirectly elected. Unlike the traditional scholarship that explains presidential power through constitutional provisions such as formal duties or policy-making authority and exclusive legislative negotiation rights, this study concentrates on the politics of presidential power. An in-depth comparative study of six Western European countries—Germany, Italy, and the inconsistent models of: - constitutional weak presidency - directly elected weak presidency Austria, Ireland, Finland and Portugal - is used as a basis to address and answer questions related to constitutional designs that may lead to a weak presidency and possibly increase the president’s political influence in national politics.

A general exposition of presidential power and presidential activism is not merely an academic endeavor; it serves to bring more insight to presidential behavior, its analysis, and its pedagogical worth. In this respect, Presidential Power and the Oval Office Astrocrat: The Rhetoric of Unlawful Orders as Informal Means of Presidential Action attempts to extend the scope of analysis beyond formal, legalistic understandings of the authority to which the President wields. In particular, it looks into the role of informal power as means of presidential activism, presents an original typology and definition of this power, and rigorously empirically examines presidential interactions within the administration to assess the informal means of presidential action as successful according to presidential preferences and analyses opportunity structure and presidential public support as those factors most instrumental to presidential political effectiveness.
